content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue a structural grade-split reorganization that shields younger children now, accepting the risk of future political reversal and unresolved fiscal exposure for retained units, or avoid this structural commitment given the severity of Poland's 2017 reversal experience?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can measure whether retained lower-grade schools meet or fall below enrollment thresholds, and can track achievement/disruption effects on consolidated upper-grade cohorts. It cannot determine in advance whether a future government will reverse the reform for political reasons, nor can it settle whether the value of near-term proximity protection outweighs the risk of eventual disruptive reversal.
content.tension: Grade-splitting (T3) protects the youngest, most commute-vulnerable children by preserving lower-grade village schools while consolidating upper grades, but this relocates rather than eliminates burden onto older students, leaves lower-grade units still fiscally exposed to national class-size minimums, and carries high reversal risk (as in Poland 2017) that could produce severe, asymmetric disruption if later abandoned.
content.title: Protecting youngest children locally versus resolving contraction structurally
value_poles: protection of the most vulnerable (younger children)
durability and non-reversibility of structural reform
fiscal sustainability under uniform national funding rules
